perturbation.original_text: 2.1 Client will pay CRO for satisfactory performance of Services as agreed in each Project-specific Addendum to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.1 Client will pay CRO based on invoices submitted, subject to internal review.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause 'as agreed in each Project-specific Addendum to this Agreement', the payment terms become vague. While the original text tied payment explicitly to agreements in project addenda, the changed text introduces 'internal review' without specifying review criteria or linking it to addenda milestones. This creates uncertainty and a potential contradiction if addenda specify payment milestones that the 'internal review' chooses to ignore or alter. This also creates a problem within section 2.4, where the 'detailed account of all time and tasks' would need to be assessed against something.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.4 CRO will provide a detailed account of all time and tasks, as against project specific task and time addenda, and will be payable within 30 days of receipt of invoice by Client.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in 2.1 removes the explicit link between payment and the 'Project-specific Addendum', replacing it with a vague 'internal review'. This directly contradicts section 2.4, which states that CRO's detailed time and task account is 'against project specific task and time addenda'. The removal of the addendum link in 2.1 means the 'internal review' lacks a defined standard to assess the detailed account against, creating a strong contradiction and potential for disputes.

perturbation.original_text: 4.2. Client may terminate this Agreement on thirty (30) days written notice without cause. In the event of termination by Client for reasons other than default by CRO, Client shall pay all sums owing to CRO, but unpaid, for work performed to date of receipt of termination notice, and all reasonable and necessary costs associated with the termination itself or to which CRO is committed to pay. In the event of termination by Client, CRO shall use all efforts to minimize any such costs, including cancelling orders and services to the extent possible.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.2. Client may terminate this Agreement on thirty (30) days written notice without cause. In the event of termination by Client for reasons other than default by CRO, Client's sole obligation is to pay for all work completed.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting 'all reasonable and necessary costs associated with the termination itself or to which CRO is committed to pay' and replacing 'pay all sums owing to CRO, but unpaid, for work performed to date of receipt of termination notice' with 'pay for all work completed', a contradiction arises. The original clause required Client to compensate CRO for termination-related costs and work performed to the termination notice date. The modified clause limits the obligation to only 'work completed'. The phrase 'work completed' contradicts the previous 'all sums owing to CRO, but unpaid, for work performed to date of receipt of termination notice' because completed work may have additional payment requirements.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4.2. Client may terminate this Agreement on thirty (30) days written notice without cause. In the event of termination by Client for reasons other than default by CRO, Client's sole obligation is to pay for all work completed.
perturbation.justification: The changed text narrows the client's obligations upon termination. The original text specified payment for 'all sums owing to CRO, but unpaid, for work performed to date of receipt of termination notice, and all reasonable and necessary costs associated with the termination itself or to which CRO is committed to pay.' The changed text limits this to 'pay for all work completed.' This creates a contradiction because costs associated with termination would no longer be covered, even if the original agreement included them, thus contradicting the original intent and creating a direct conflict on the payable amount upon termination.

perturbation.original_text: 5.4. Notwithstanding any other provision of this Agreement, each party's total liability in respect of damages under this Agreement, any regulation or common law shall be limited to the sum of all amounts received from Client in terms of this Agreement; provided, however, that this limitation shall not apply with respect to any claims arising out of or relating to clause 6 (Inventions and Proprietary Information), indemnification obligations or damages arising from a party's gross negligence or willful misconduct.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.4. Notwithstanding any other provision of this Agreement, each party's total liability in respect of damages under this Agreement, any regulation or common law shall be limited to the sum of all amounts received from Client in terms of this Agreement; provided, however, that this limitation shall not apply with respect to any claims arising out of or relating to clause 6 (Inventions and Proprietary Information) or damages arising from a party's gross negligence or willful misconduct.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'indemnification obligations' from the exceptions to the liability cap, a contradiction is created with sections 5.6 and 5.7. These sections explicitly state indemnification obligations for both CRO and Client. With the change, the overall liability is capped even with the indemnification exceptions, creating uncertainty about the enforceability of the indemnification clauses specified in sections 5.6 and 5.7.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.6. CRO will defend, indemnify and hold harmless Client and its directors, officers, employees and agents from and against all liabilities, costs and expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees and court costs) arising from any third party claim, action or lawsuit or other proceeding which is attributable to any negligent or willful act or omission or breach of this Agreement on the part of CRO or any of its agents or employees in the course of performing CRO's obligations hereunder.
perturbation.justification: The original text in Section 5.4 explicitly excluded 'indemnification obligations' from the liability cap, aligning with Section 5.6, which mandates CRO to 'defend, indemnify and hold harmless Client'. The changed text removes 'indemnification obligations' from the exceptions to the liability cap in Section 5.4. This creates a direct contradiction, because now the liability cap applies even when indemnification is required under Section 5.6, creating uncertainty and conflict about the enforceability of the indemnification obligations.
